reason: The Hebrew people (Israelites) are consistently portrayed in a favorable light throughout this passage. They are depicted as God's chosen people who receive divine protection and favor. Specific favorable elements include: (1) Divine protection in the plagues—the land of Goshen where the Israelites dwelt is spared from hail, and they have light during the plague of darkness while Egyptians experience darkness; (2) Obedience to God—the text states the Israelites 'went and did as the Lord commanded Moses and Aaron'; (3) Gaining favor in Egyptian eyes—'the Lord gave the people favor in the sight of the Egyptians,' resulting in gifts of gold and silver; (4) Moses is described as 'very great in the land of Egypt'; (5) They follow God's instructions regarding the Passover carefully. The overall narrative shows them as faithful, protected, and ultimately delivered from slavery.
